title: RetroLook - Dragonfire (1992)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cJwXjAKA0-k

Daniel (@dungeondive) takes a RetroLook at Dragonfire, a 1992 HeroQuest wannabe from Heartbreaker Games — and he's honest: it's not a hidden gem, it's a "weird game" that's better than you'd think but still not good. Yet, oddly, it contains nearly everything he looks for in a dungeon crawl (bar solo play). What he genuinely admires is the rulebook's note to the game master — explicitly told *not* to play against the players but to make the monsters fun and exciting, and to invent their own adventures. A non-adversarial overlord, which he always appreciates.

Play is simple HeroQuest fare: explore door-to-door as the GM reveals each room, search for traps and hidden items, draw equipment and magic, fight through to a boss. Four identical heroes (differing only in card colour) level white→red→black; three monster types (orcs, trolls, dark lords) differ mostly by colour, with the dark lords attacking in chess-like directions. An events deck lets the GM spring traps. Components are flimsy, but the cards feel nice. He keeps it largely for the previous owner's charming retro Sharpie paint job, and as a distillation of everything he loves about the genre — a reminder the market could use a simple game done well.

----

Daniel keeps Dragonfire partly for the charming paint job a previous owner left on the minis — he rarely strips used figures, treating them as gaming heirlooms. Do you keep or strip the paint on second-hand games?
